docs.00cb7f1a05: Below, “X” means “don’t care”   either input value produces the required transition.
docs.00cee4c400: Swap that row into row k.
docs.01d3584bd1: In 74Sim: Wire QA to CLK2 on the 74176 to get a straightforward 0–9 BCD count. Drive CLK1 with your input clock. The four outputs QA–QD represent the BCD digit with weights 1–2–4–8 respectively.
docs.01fb87976d: Supply: 5V ±0.25V (±5%), a regulated supply is best. Add a 0.1 μF decoupling capacitor near each IC.
docs.03c0435627: 74HCT is a variant of 74HC whose input thresholds accept the voltage levels produced by 74LS TTL outputs. Use it when mixing 74LS and 74HC logic in the same circuit.
docs.048603fea2: A full list of 74 series chips can be found at build electronic-circuits.com. 74Sim supports the chips shown in the chip picker in the toolbar.
docs.049ca65375: GND (ground) is on the bottom-right pin
docs.07fbe77449: Pass 1 (LED on, stamp R = 33 Ω + VF Norton): The LED's bulk conductance is now GLED = 1/33 ≈ 0.0303 S between net 2 and GND, and a current source ILED = VF × GLED ≈ 0.0606 A is injected to subtract the forward drop:
docs.09f82ff0aa: Main grid: columns of 5 holes (rows A to E on top, F to J on bottom). All holes in the same column and the same half are connected together. The center channel separates the two halves.
docs.0c0fbb9218: The table below shows every common two input gate. A = first input, B = second input, 0 = LOW, 1 = HIGH.
docs.0c482b1e5c: Net 2: the node between the resistor and the LED's anode.
docs.0da1d6cbaf: Power rails: long rows along each side, marked + and −. Each rail runs the full length of the board. Connect your power supply here and tap power from any hole along it.
docs.0da8b7f158: The images below show an AND gate with floating inputs on the left, and the same gate with pull down resistors on the right:
docs.0e1d877c12: A breadboard is a reusable prototyping board made up of a grid of holes, with metal spring clips electronically connecting sections of the board, no soldering required.
docs.0e9ab9bd75: Setup time (tsu): the minimum time before the active clock edge during which the data input (D, J, K, or T) must be stable.
docs.102068b3fd: Tristate output disabled; open collector output off.
docs.10f202be99: Logic gate inputs are very high impedance typically megohms for TTL, gigohms for CMOS. That high impedance is what makes them cheap to drive, but it also means the pin has no preferred voltage of its own. A few nanoamps of leakage, a millivolt of induced noise from the 60 Hz mains, or the capacitance between the wire and your finger is enough to swing the pin across the logic threshold.
docs.11eca4fa04: Save circuits to your computer, or load examples from the archive to see how the pieces fit together.
docs.126615bc78: The two families behave very differently when a pin is left floating, and the difference matters.
docs.136f79b625: Edge triggered flip flops have three timing parameters, all measured against the active clock edge, that bound the maximum reliable clock frequency of a synchronous system:
docs.13ce3ea41a: Supply: 5V ±0.5V, a regulated supply is recommended.
docs.13ced64be9: A standard push pull output actively drives its pin both HIGH and LOW. An open collector output is different: it can only pull the pin LOW (by connecting the collector of an internal transistor to the output pin). When the output is “HIGH,” the transistor is simply off and the pin floats.
docs.145dd252e9: The rule for both families is the same, only the consequence of breaking it differs: drive every input to a defined level. Tie unused gate inputs to GND or VCC directly (no resistor needed for permanently unused inputs), and use pull ups or pull downs on inputs that are sometimes driven and sometimes not buttons, jumpers, tri state buses.
docs.14b6797dfd: M. M. Mano and C. R. Kime, Logic and Computer Design Fundamentals, 3rd ed. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Prentice Hall, 2004, p. 73. Also: Wikipedia contributors, “NAND gate.” Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NAND_gate.
docs.15aabbbcc9: All chips are evaluated in placement order. Each chip reads its input pin voltages, computes its logic function, and writes output drive states to pinDriveStates.
docs.173fa7b2f7: Pull down and pull up resistors
docs.1790277503: Backward Euler is unconditionally stable. The capacitor cannot oscillate or blow up regardless of how large dt is. This is a key reason it's the default choice for circuit simulators.
docs.194c638400: New to breadboards? Read How a Breadboard Works.
docs.1aaf4666fe: For 74LS only: an undriven chip input pin receives a 100 kΩ pull up to VCC, causing it to read HIGH, matching real 74LS behaviour. 74HC, 74HCT, and 74LVC inputs receive no pull up and stay truly floating.
docs.1ac11093c4: G<sub>eq</sub> is stamped exactly like a resistor between the capacitor's two pins; I<sub>eq</sub> is injected into z as a current source from one pin to the other. After the solve, V<sub>prev</sub> is updated with the new pin to pin voltage and the cycle repeats next step.
docs.1ac4507f8f: The loop exits early (after at least 2 passes) when no chip changes any output, meaning the circuit has reached a stable DC operating point.
docs.1c494a444a: 74LS (TTL): Use 1.4 kΩ. LS inputs source a small current when held LOW; a lower-value resistor is needed to overcome it and keep the pin below VIL.
docs.1d3face005: The 74HC373 octal D latch (8 transparent latches with a shared LE and tristate /OE) is the classic example. The 74HC75 contains four D latches in two pairs. Don’t confuse the 74HC373 with the 74HC374, which is the edge triggered D flip flop equivalent in the same package style; the ’373 is level sensitive, the ’374 is edge triggered.
docs.1dc26d5f76: A D flip flop is also the simplest way to build a divide by-2: tie D to /Q and Q toggles on every clock edge, producing an output at half the input frequency.
docs.1e02a55def: Solve the linear system for every node voltage at this instant, then use those voltages to update gate logic and (for transient effects) to advance time, then repeat until nothing changes or the user stops.
docs.1f2e75c5ae: Choosing the value is a tradeoff. A smaller resistor (1 10 kΩ) gives stronger noise immunity but draws more current when the input is driven the other way; a larger one (47 100 kΩ) saves power but leaves the pin more susceptible to interference on long wires. The right value depends on which family you are using:
docs.1f8290bf46: Static sensitivity: like all CMOS, LVC inputs are ESD sensitive. Tie unused inputs to VCC or GND.
docs.1fb3829bef: In 74Sim: Wire several open collector outputs (e.g. 74LS05 inverters) to a common node and add a pull up resistor to VCC. The node will read LOW whenever any output is active, implementing Wired AND in the simulator exactly as in hardware.
docs.218e2c534d: A Schmitt trigger is an input circuit with hysteresis: it switches HIGH at one voltage threshold (VT+) but does not switch back LOW until the input falls to a lower threshold (VT−). The gap between the two thresholds gives the Schmitt trigger its noise immunity a slowly rising or noisy signal will not cause multiple rapid toggles.
docs.2270c9a69d: This two pass scheme is mathematically equivalent to a single Newton Raphson iteration of the standard SPICE diode model started from a good initial guess (everything off), and is accurate enough for the 0 to 5 V range these chips operate at. The constants are taken from js/simulator.js: LED_VF, LED_R_INTERNAL, DIODE_VF, DIODE_R_INTERNAL, and LED_G_OFF / DIODE_G_OFF.
docs.22c015ad3c: Pick whichever fits where you are:
docs.22cdec5d04: On a schematic the active clock edge is drawn as a small triangle (▹) at the clock input. A bubble in front of the triangle (▹ with ○) means the flip flop is falling edge triggered. In datasheet timing diagrams the active edge is the moment at which D, J, K, or T are sampled and against which setup and hold times are measured.
docs.2362873293: A totem pole output is the standard output structure used in most TTL and CMOS logic gates. It gets its name from the physical arrangement of two transistors stacked vertically on the output pin: one transistor pulls the pin HIGH (sources current to the load) while the other pulls it LOW (sinks current from the load). Only one transistor is on at a time, so the output actively drives to a definite HIGH or LOW level in both directions.
docs.248341c678: 74Sim is an open source simulator for 74 series logic chips. You build circuits on a virtual breadboard, wire up switches and LEDs, and watch the logic run as you flip inputs.
docs.2601e93a50: No stamp at all; the pin is electrically absent. Open collector pins in this state receive an implicit 4.7 kΩ pull up to VCC.
docs.2755357ab9: Pick from the 74HC, 74LS, 74HCT, and 74LVC families: gates, flip flops, counters, shift registers, multiplexers, arithmetic chips, and bus transceivers.
docs.2806a64d81: VCC (power) is on the top-left pin
docs.2963ef9b39: A latch is a one bit memory element whose behavior depends on the level of its enable input rather than on a clock edge. While the enable is asserted, the output follows the data input continuously the latch is “open.” When the enable is de asserted the latch “closes” and freezes the last value it saw. Because latches are level sensitive they are asynchronous: the output can change at any moment while enabled, not only at a single instant per clock cycle. For this reason synchronous designs almost always use flip flops in place of latches.
docs.297725b300: If setup or hold are violated for example because the upstream combinational logic was too slow the flip flop may enter metastability: Q sits at an indeterminate voltage between the valid HIGH and LOW levels for an unbounded time before resolving randomly to 0 or 1. Every synchronous design has a maximum clock period given by tCO + tlogic + tsu; exceeding it is the most common source of intermittent failures in digital systems.
docs.2a6b5eaa26: An excitation table is the inverse of the characteristic table: given a desired transition from Q to Q+, it lists the input values that produce it. Excitation tables are the bridge from a state diagram (which specifies the desired transitions) to the gate level inputs needed to drive a flip flop. They are the second step in synthesis, immediately after writing down the state table.
docs.2bf94a02df: Schmitt trigger inverters (e.g. 74HC14) are commonly used to clean up slow or bouncy signals such as the output of an RC oscillator or a mechanical button before feeding them into clocked logic. In the capabilities page the 74HC14 is noted as a way to debounce button presses.
docs.2c9fa04ef9: For each column k, find the row with the largest absolute value in column k at or below the diagonal. This is the pivot row.
docs.31dc77256f: No extra gate IC is required: the wiring itself performs the logic. Wired AND is widely used on I²C buses (SDA and SCL lines) and on interrupt request lines where multiple sources must share a single signal without bus contention. Only open collector or open drain outputs are safe to wire together this way connecting push pull outputs directly causes a short circuit between the HIGH driver and the LOW driver.
docs.326eef33b3: Compute derived quantities: per branch resistor, capacitor, and diode currents; per net current sums.
docs.335bed46dd: Same, with active LOW signals (e.g. push button bounce)
docs.33d0158bf0: Capacitor pin to pin voltage is clamped to a maximum change of 0.25 V per step in _updateCapacitorState, producing a visually gradual exponential charge curve over many steps even when dt is large.
docs.34339cb3b6: In 74Sim: The ULN2003 has no VCC pin only GND needs to be connected. Each channel is INVERTING in the open collector sense: drive an input HIGH and the matching output sinks hard to GND (modeled as a strong few tens of ohms pull down); drive it LOW and the output floats. Wire the load between VCC and the output pin, exactly as you would with real hardware.
docs.356fec07c9: A logic gate is a circuit that performs a Boolean operation on one or more binary inputs and produces a single binary output. All 74 series logic is built from combinations of these primitive gates.
docs.35c75d14f3: The structural side is a plain JavaScript object. Here is the real definition of the 7400 (Quad 2 input NAND):
docs.399f6f77f7: Clock to-Q propagation delay (tCO or tpd): the time from the active clock edge until Q updates to its new value.
docs.3b790ffd89: A controller device (master slave) flip flop chains two latches back to back driven by opposite phases of the clock. On the rising clock edge the leader latch captures the input; on the falling edge the follower latch copies the leader’s output to Q. The result is a fully edge triggered element with no transparency during the clock cycle. This architecture prevents the “ones catching” problem of single stage JK designs (where a brief HIGH on J during the clock’s active phase would erroneously set Q) and is the internal structure of most 74 series flip flop ICs.
docs.3c71e89f80: Why this matters in synchronous design: because the latch is transparent during the entire HIGH phase of EN, any glitch on D between the rising and falling edge of EN can ripple through to Q. Replacing the latch with an edge triggered D flip flop confines D → Q transfer to a single instant per clock cycle, which is what synchronous timing analysis assumes.
docs.3cc9d4b16c: The result x is one voltage for every non GND net. Downstream code reads these voltages and classifies each chip input pin as logic HIGH (V ≥ V<sub>TH</sub>) or LOW (V < V<sub>TH</sub>) using the family dependent threshold.
docs.3d7c235b9d: There is no widely used standalone T flip flop in the 74 series designers build one from a JK or D when needed. T flip flops appear inside ripple counters such as the 74HC93 and 74HC393, where each stage toggles on the falling edge of the previous stage’s Q to perform binary division.
docs.3d97bcba72: Chip logic and the MNA solver are mutually dependent: gate outputs change A and z, and the solved voltages in turn determine the next round of gate inputs. To resolve this without infinite recursion, the simulator runs an iterative fixed point loop:
docs.3e55a3fa57: Unverified chips display a yellow warning banner at the top of their chip reference page (here in the docs) and in the chip info panel inside the simulator. There is no "View Datasheet" link for these chips.
docs.3e591c6e04: Hold time (th): the minimum time after the active clock edge during which the data input must remain stable.
docs.3e5ef24abd: DC steady state is correct. When Vnow = Vprev, the Ieq current source exactly cancels the current that Geq would otherwise draw. Net DC current through the capacitor is zero, so it correctly blocks DC.
docs.3f387efa31: Wired OR is less common in TTL/CMOS logic than Wired AND because most bus standards use active LOW signalling, but the concept is important in emitter coupled logic (ECL) and in some mixed signal systems. The term is also used informally to describe any situation where multiple signals are tied together through diodes so that any HIGH input forces the bus HIGH.
docs.3f9fe975ce: AND: output is HIGH only when all inputs are HIGH.
docs.41c3ca9af9: XNOR (exclusive NOR): output is HIGH when inputs are equal. The inverse of XOR; used in comparators.
docs.41fe119b4c: The letters between "74" and the function number indicate which family a chip belongs to. Families differ in transistor technology, supply voltage, speed, power consumption, and input/output behavior.
docs.4350dd05e6: 74HC / 74HCT (CMOS): Use 140 kΩ. CMOS inputs draw almost no current, so a large resistor holds the pin reliably without wasting power.
docs.443ce21ba0: A flip flop is a one bit memory element that samples its inputs only at the active edge of a clock signal either the rising edge (LOW → HIGH) or the falling edge (HIGH → LOW). For the rest of the clock period the inputs are ignored and Q is held constant. This is the defining difference between a flip flop and a latch: a latch is “open” for an entire half cycle, a flip flop captures at one instant per cycle.
docs.455da04af8: One detail specific to 74Sim: _updateCapacitorState clamps the voltage change per step to MAX_CAP_DV = 0.25 V. This is purely cosmetic. It stretches the visible RC charge curve onto many frames so users can see a capacitor charging instead of snapping to its final voltage in one tick. The math underneath is still backward Euler.
docs.48a28709df: Under the hood, 74Sim ships a custom SPICE class analog engine. This page explains how a circuit on the breadboard becomes math, and how that math becomes the voltages, currents, and LED brightness you see on the screen. The top of the page is conceptual; later sections drill into the equations and constants used by the real code.
docs.48c37aaa5c: Wikipedia contributors, “Schmitt trigger,” Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schmitt_trigger. (Accessed: May 4, 2026).
docs.49f289828c: The D flip flop is the workhorse of modern digital logic. It has a single data input D and one clock input. On each active clock edge it samples D and copies it to Q; for the rest of the clock period Q is held. Conceptually it is just a D latch sampled at the clock edge instead of held open across the clock HIGH phase.
docs.4b656c00bf: Pins are numbered counter clockwise starting from pin 1 at the bottom-left
docs.4d22eec599: Two important properties fall out of this:
docs.4e1dbf5349: JK flip flops are the foundation of ripple counters and shift registers. Common chips include the 74HC73 (dual JK with clear), the 74HC76 (dual JK with preset and clear), and the 74HC112 (dual JK, falling edge triggered).
docs.513ed0b03c: Open any chip to see its pinout and a short guide on how it works and where it gets used.
docs.526c8967e4: Wired OR is the complementary technique: two or more open emitter (open source) outputs share a wire with a pull down resistor to GND. The line is pulled HIGH whenever any driver is active, which is equivalent to an OR gate applied to the active HIGH sense of each driver.
docs.53d5ae44f6: Iterative gate + MNA loop: evaluate chip logic, record output drive states, solve the MNA system, and repeat until the circuit settles (up to 30 passes).
docs.54686a250e: Concretely, when any component is placed, moved, wired, or toggled, 74Sim runs the following pipeline synchronously before the next frame is rendered:
docs.557748a32c: The JK flip flop is the edge triggered descendant of the SR latch. It eliminates the forbidden S = R = 1 state by redefining J = K = 1 as toggle: Q flips to its complement on each active clock edge. With the four input combinations it can perform every primitive operation a one bit element can do hold, set, reset, and toggle making it the most flexible flip flop type.
docs.5683b38781: When a circuit contains capacitors or clock components, the simulator starts a real time loop that advances simulation time in 20 ms wall clock intervals (targeting 50 FPS):
docs.56b5475969: Piecewise linear two pass model. See Nonlinear elements.
docs.580558fbcf: An inverter (NOT gate) flips a single bit: a HIGH input produces a LOW output and vice versa. A hex inverter is a single chip package containing six independent inverters, hence the prefix “hex.” The 74LS04 and 74HC04 are the classic examples. Each gate is completely independent; unused gates should have their inputs tied to GND.
docs.595e28161e: Per net current totals are aggregated by summing the absolute branch currents touching each net. These totals drive the current flow visualisation, set LED brightness (proportional to forward current), and trigger overcurrent warnings when a single net carries more than its rail's share.
docs.5ad2075dd3: Set when J = 1 and Q = 0; hold when K = 0 and Q = 1
docs.5be9ea5677: Once A and z are assembled, the unknown node voltages x are obtained by solving the linear system. 74Sim uses Gaussian elimination with partial column pivoting on a fresh Float64Array backed N×N matrix per pass:
docs.5cf3fb706d: 74Sim note: the simulator's VCC rail is fixed at 5V. A real 74LVC part powered at 5V would be destroyed. In 74Sim, the LVC family selection models LVC's logic level behavior (TTL compatible thresholds, low output impedance, high speed, no floating bias) running on the simulator's 5V supply rather than a literal 3.3V system.
docs.5d2a04ac2e: Breadboard internal connections (the 5 hole columns and power rail rows) are implicit edges traversed by the BFS.
docs.5dd9451cf7: Leaving a logic input unconnected is not the same as tying it to GND. A pin with nothing driving it is floating: its voltage is set by whatever stray currents and capacitive coupling happen to reach it, not by your circuit. The result is an input that reads HIGH, LOW, or oscillates between the two depending on the time of day, the length of the wire, and whether your hand is nearby.
docs.606b4f56a8: M. M. Mano and C. R. Kime, Logic and Computer Design Fundamentals, 3rd ed. Upper Saddle River, NJ: Prentice Hall, 2004, p. 73. Also: Wikipedia contributors, “NOR gate.” Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NOR_gate.
docs.607dd735bb: Each tick increments simTime by the current adaptive time step dt and calls evaluate().
docs.612c6ef1a4: An initial MNA pass is run before any chip evaluates, so feedback wires (e.g. Q→CK on a ripple counter) already carry correct voltages from the previous simulation step.
docs.6159f60a22: A tristate output has three possible states instead of two: HIGH, LOW, and high impedance (Hi Z). In the Hi Z state the output is electrically disconnected from the bus and neither sources nor sinks current. This allows many chips to share a single data bus: only one chip is enabled at a time, while all others are in Hi Z so they do not interfere. Most bus driver and buffer chips (e.g. 74HC244, 74HC245) use tristate outputs controlled by an active LOW Output Enable (/OE) pin.
docs.615d37adad: The totem pole structure gives logic gates low output impedance in both states, which means they can source and sink current quickly and drive long traces or many inputs without significant voltage sag. The tradeoff is that totem pole outputs cannot be tied directly together: if one output is driving HIGH and another is driving LOW on the same wire, the two transistors fight each other and cause a short circuit current spike. Use open collector or tristate outputs when multiple drivers must share a bus.
docs.61a1e93ad8: Current is not solved for directly. It is derived from the now known node voltages on a per branch basis after the linear solve completes:
docs.6241d4a622: Toggle when T = 1; hold when T = 0
docs.628dec962b: Every wire segment merges the two holes it spans into the same net.
docs.629252c0c3: Note: Because backward Euler is unconditionally stable, the simulator can use a fairly coarse time step (up to 50 ms) without numerical blow up. The adaptive policy in the time stepping section keeps the curve smooth without wasting CPU at steady state.
docs.62e2140263: The behavioural side lives in a per chip evaluator function. For a combinational chip like the 7400, the evaluator simply iterates the gates array, reads each input pin as a bit (HIGH if the solved voltage is above the family threshold V<sub>TH</sub>, otherwise LOW), computes the boolean, and writes the result to the output pin's drive state.
docs.63c323f262: Wikipedia contributors, “Wired logic connection,” Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wired_logic_connection. (Accessed: May 10, 2026).
docs.63d04f4eb6: If you have access to an official datasheet for an unverified chip, or if you notice that a chip's simulation behavior does not match real hardware, please submit a bug report. Include the chip part number, a link to the datasheet (or the PDF itself), and a description of what is wrong. Your report will help improve accuracy for everyone.
docs.64f4cc1f7f: A register is a group of flip flops that store multiple bits simultaneously, all clocked together. A typical 8 bit register holds one byte of data and latches it on each rising clock edge. Registers appear in CPUs to hold operands and results, and in digital circuits wherever parallel data needs to be captured and held. Shift register chips such as the 74HC595 (serial in, parallel out) and 74HC165 (parallel in, serial out) are common 74 series examples.
docs.658d4e0783: To make all of this concrete, here is a hand solve of the simplest non trivial circuit: a 5 V supply through a 330 Ω resistor to a red LED to ground.
docs.66ec274ac0: Solving (and ignoring the 10<sup>−9</sup> S leak) gives V<sub>1</sub> ≈ 4.93 V and V<sub>2</sub> ≈ 2.30 V. The LED current is then computed in the post solve current pass:
docs.6756555dc5: Keyboard shortcuts available while the simulator is open.
docs.67f71d3ede: Recommendation: For most new projects, choose 74HC. It works from 2 to 6V (battery friendly), has excellent noise immunity, and very low idle power. Switch to 74HCT when you need TTL compatible inputs, to 74LS for existing 5V TTL systems, or to 74LVC for 3.3V designs and high speed level translation.
docs.69ee1d4475: which is the realistic forward current you would expect from a 5 V supply, a 330 Ω series resistor, and a 2 V red LED. Every quantity on the screen for this circuit  the voltage at each net, the current through each branch, the LED's brightness  is computed by exactly this two pass procedure.
docs.6a9c8bce07: The front of the breadboard shows labeled rows and columns of holes. The back reveals the metal clips that make the connections.
docs.6be3b6f2e8: Most 74 series flip flops include asynchronous Preset (PRE, PR, or SD for “set direct”) and Clear (CLR, MR for “master reset,” or RD for “reset direct”) inputs that override the clock entirely. These inputs are usually active LOW (signified by an overbar in the datasheet) and force Q to a known state regardless of D, J, K, T, or CLK. Asserting both at once produces an indeterminate state and is forbidden, just like S = R = 1 on the SR latch.
docs.6c70bd224f: After the matrix is upper triangular, back substitute from the bottom row up to recover x.
docs.6c8bfc4a14: A chip in 74Sim has two completely separate representations: a structural description (pure data) and a behavioural evaluator (a function). The simulator does not know what a NAND is from the data alone; it only knows that a particular chip type has an evaluator that, when called, reads its input pin voltages and writes drive states to its output pins.
docs.6d410816db: Inputs are TTL compatible (V<sub>IH</sub> ≥ 2.0V, V<sub>IL</sub> ≤ 0.8V) so they accept 74LS, 74HC, and 74HCT outputs directly.
docs.6ed68ac820: Outputs can sink and source ~24 mA, much stronger than 74HC.
docs.6f2f793bf5: If any drive state changed, another MNA pass is run and the chip loop repeats. The loop runs for at most 30 iterations.
docs.6f37c71b80: Inputs float high to logic 1 when unconnected. Connect unused inputs to +Vs in real circuits.
docs.70b7eb1f09: Net 1: the VCC node one side of the 330 Ω resistor and the VCC rail's Norton source.
docs.70faf337f2: Hysteresis is the property of a system whose output depends on the history of the input, not just its instantaneous value. In digital logic the term almost always refers to input switching thresholds: the voltage at which the input switches from LOW to HIGH (VT+) is intentionally set higher than the voltage at which it switches back from HIGH to LOW (VT−). The gap between the two thresholds — the hysteresis band — must be crossed in the opposite direction before the output changes state again.
docs.72b65aa218: So A is 2×2 and z is length 2.
docs.736d1c2863: The chip exists in the 74 series number space but was never widely produced.
docs.764398bcca: V<sub>TH</sub> is the internal compare value used by the simulator when classifying a solved node voltage as logic HIGH (≥ V<sub>TH</sub>) or logic LOW (< V<sub>TH</sub>). In real hardware the actual switching point varies device to device; V<sub>TH</sub> is the typical midpoint.
docs.788a5760ee: Propagation delay: ~3 ns. Max frequency: ~100 MHz.
docs.7a200dd07e: The S = R = 1 case is “forbidden” because both outputs are momentarily forced to the same level, breaking the Q, /Q complementarity. When the inputs return to 0 0 the next state depends on a race between the two gates and is not predictable. The D latch below was invented to make this state physically impossible.
docs.7b0655e448: Edge triggering is what makes synchronous digital design possible. Every storage element in a synchronous system updates at the same instant, so the result of one clock cycle becomes the input to the next without the “ones catching” or transparency problems that plague level sensitive designs.
docs.7ba2df4c8b: 74LVC is a low voltage CMOS family intended for 3.3V (and lower) digital systems. Most LVC parts have 5V tolerant inputs, which lets them act as level translators between modern 3.3V logic and legacy 5V 74HC / 74LS systems. Compared to 74HC, LVC is much faster and has stronger output drive.
docs.7c7488e109: G = 100 S (0.01 Ω) between the two end nodes, effectively an ideal conductor.
docs.7d257e3535: Evaluate outputs: light LEDs, update 7 segment segments.
docs.7db090246f: NAND: NOT/AND; output is LOW only when all inputs are HIGH. Universal gate: any logic function can be built from NANDs alone.
docs.7e794c7565: 74Sim uses the backward Euler companion:
docs.7ea9559fc0: A bi quinary counter counts to ten by cascading two independent stages: a divide by-2 stage (“bi”) and a divide by-5 stage (“quinary”). Each stage is a straightforward binary counter, and together they produce a BCD (Binary Coded Decimal) count from 0 to 9 before resetting. The name simply reflects the factoring: 2 × 5 = 10.
docs.7f4fb48d41: Build the netlist: flood fill the breadboard to identify nets.
docs.801a108081: Propagation delay: ~10 ns per gate. Max frequency: ~35 MHz.
docs.8045ffcb49: Nonlinear elements: the two pass diode/LED model
docs.819963be14: Bus side data capture, address latching (e.g. 8085 multiplexed bus)
docs.8429d19836: Wikipedia contributors, “Flip flop (electronics) § Master–slave edge triggered D flip flop,” Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flip-flop_(electronics)#Master%E2%80%93slave_edge triggered_D_flip flop. (Accessed: May 4, 2026).
docs.846385ed96:
Pass 1, re stamp the conducting ones. Each forward biased device is re stamped as a bulk resistance plus a Norton current source that subtracts its forward voltage drop:
  • LEDs: R = 33 Ω, VF = 2.0 V.
  • Silicon diodes: R = 10 Ω, VF = 0.7 V.
Solve again. The result is the final node voltages with each diode contributing its drop.
docs.8a5846bb63: Wired AND is a technique where two or more open collector (or open drain) outputs are connected directly together on a shared wire with a single pull up resistor to VCC. Because any active output can sink the line to GND, the shared wire is LOW whenever any driver is active identical to the behavior of an AND gate applied to the active LOW sense of each driver.
docs.8a7449bf53: In the 74 series the two stages share a chip but are driven by separate clock inputs, giving you flexibility in how you wire them. The classic configuration ties the divide by-2 output (QA) into the clock of the divide by-5 stage; the combined Q output is then standard 8-4-2-1 BCD. Alternatively you can drive the divide by-5 stage first and feed its output into the divide by-2, which swaps the bit weighting to 2-1-8-4 (sometimes called “biquinary” or “5-2” mode). Both output orders count the same 0–9 sequence; only the pin weights differ.
docs.8d3ad7134e: The JK characteristic equation is worth memorising: it captures all four operations (00 hold, 01 reset, 10 set, 11 toggle) in a single Boolean expression and is the canonical example used in nearly every digital logic textbook.
docs.8fb8f0f201: Real diodes are exponential (the Shockley equation), but MNA needs a linear system. SPICE solves this with Newton Raphson iteration, which repeatedly linearises the diode around the current voltage estimate. 74Sim uses a simplified equivalent: two successive solves per evaluate() pass, switching the diode/LED stamps between an off form and an on form based on the first solve's result.
docs.91f25c631e: Conductance G = 1/R<sub>OUT</sub> from the pin's net to GND, pulling it low. No current source. The pin can only sink, not source.
docs.9368f354a5: Every SPICE style simulator does the same three things in a loop:
docs.93a2682f91: Each component contributes to A and z by a standard stamp, a small fixed pattern of additions:
docs.9675e03c4b: Turn the circuit into a graph: identify every electrically connected group of holes (a net) and every component that bridges two nets.
docs.96a73fd153: The fix is to give the pin a defined idle level using a weak resistor to a rail. A pull down resistor to GND holds the input LOW until something actively drives it HIGH; a pull up to VCC does the opposite. The resistor is weak enough (typically 1 kΩ to 100 kΩ) that any real driver wins easily, but strong enough to swamp the stray currents that cause floating.
docs.97b9ee1e77: Outputs can sink and source ~4 mA (logic) or up to ~20 mA (max).
docs.9889dcd5a2: Norton equivalent: G = 1/2.5 Ω (0.4 S), I = 5 V × G = 2 A. Equivalent to a perfect 5 V source with 2.5 Ω series resistance, giving a 2 A short circuit limit.
docs.999bb10a7e: Capacitors: I = G<sub>eq</sub> × (V<sub>now</sub> − V<sub>prev</sub>), a discrete approximation of I = C × dV/dt over one time step.
docs.9a046ec59b: Eliminate column k from all rows below k by subtracting an appropriate multiple of row k.
docs.9a1f52ccd4: Idle power is very low, comparable to 74HC.
docs.9a5a5a6e35: The part is obscure or discontinued and no public datasheet PDF exists.
docs.9cc8d8a49e: Backward Euler companion model: Geq = C/dt as a conductance, plus a Norton current source Ieq = Geq × Vprev injected into z. See How capacitance is modelled.
docs.9d49fbb259: Click any example below to open it directly in the simulator. Made something cool? Submit your project to the archive.
docs.9f6d1e61b6: 74LVC is commonly used as a one way level translator from 5V TTL/CMOS down to 3.3V logic: the 5V tolerant LVC input accepts the 5V signal and the LVC output drives the 3.3V side. Going the other way (3.3V LVC output into 5V 74HC input) requires a dedicated level shifter, since 3.3V is below VIH for 74HC.
docs.a1b9c1013f: Each net is annotated with isVCC / isGND when any of its holes belongs to a power rail.
docs.a26aa8971b: Must tie to V<sub>CC</sub> or GND
docs.a378b982fd: Inputs have very high impedance. All unused inputs must be tied to +Vs or GND.
docs.a38da223e7: Component boundary pairs: 2-pin passives (resistors, LEDs, capacitors, diodes) act as net boundaries. Their two pins are never merged. The component sits between two distinct nets and carries current across them during the solve.
docs.a39decb70d: Wikipedia contributors, “Flip flop (electronics) § JK flip flop,” Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flip-flop_(electronics)#JK_flip flop. (Accessed: May 4, 2026).
docs.a415b18965: Some chips in 74Sim have not been verified against an official manufacturer datasheet. Their pinouts and simulation behavior are based on best effort community definitions and may not match real hardware.
docs.a47d04e34b: NOR: NOT/OR; output is HIGH only when all inputs are LOW. Also a universal gate.
docs.a4eb476d7e: Have each component contribute a small pattern of numbers, called a stamp, into a single global linear system Ax = z. Resistors stamp a conductance; voltage sources stamp a current; capacitors stamp a time discretised approximation; chip outputs stamp a Norton equivalent driver.
docs.a51c197b2a: All other characteristics are the same as 74HC.
docs.a55082e9aa: Solving gives V<sub>1</sub> ≈ 5.00 V and V<sub>2</sub> ≈ 5.00 V. The LED's anode (V<sub>2</sub>) minus cathode (0 V) is 5 V  greater than V<sub>F</sub> = 2.0 V, so the LED is forward biased.
docs.a67be1e155: Wikipedia contributors, “Wired logic connection § Wired OR,” Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wired_logic_connection. (Accessed: May 10, 2026).
docs.a6bc5e48d0: The don’t cares are why the JK is so attractive for counter and state machine design: every transition has at least one don’t-care input, which collapses to simpler combinational logic when minimised with a Karnaugh map. The D excitation table has no don’t cares (D = Q+ always), which is why D flip flops dominate FPGA and ASIC design where the next state logic is generated automatically by a synthesis tool.
docs.a717534bea: An SR latch can be built from two cross coupled NOR gates (active HIGH inputs, as in the truth table above) or two cross coupled NAND gates (active LOW inputs, often labelled /S and /R). The Example Projects section includes an SR latch built from 74LS02 NOR gates.
docs.a7180934f4: Drop chips onto a breadboard and wire them up with switches, buttons, LEDs, and resistors.
docs.a73530d515: LEDs and diodes: if forward biased, I = (V<sub>anode</sub> − V<sub>cathode</sub> − V<sub>F</sub>) / R<sub>internal</sub>; otherwise the leakage current through the off state conductance (~1 μA).
docs.a795500281: If nothing is broken but you would like a chip added, a pinout corrected, or something in the docs or simulator done differently, use the Request a Change form instead.
docs.a888d31db7: Why this works: applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at every non reference node says "the sum of currents leaving the node equals zero." Each branch contributes a current that, by Ohm's law, is a linear function of node voltages (I = G × ΔV for a resistor, plus an offset for any current source). Collecting the equations for all N nodes gives exactly the matrix form above.
docs.a8e67c0b89: Want to just try it? Open the simulator and start placing chips.
docs.ab7ed941c2: The 74176 is the classic presettable bi quinary decade counter. Its QA pin is the divide by-2 output and QB–QD form the divide by-5 group. Feeding QA into CLK2 gives a standard BCD count on QA–QD. The architecture appears in many frequency division and decimal counting applications: BCD adders, digital clocks, event counters, and frequency meters all rely on decade counters built this way.
docs.ad6660615a: A bufferis a gate with a single input whose output equals its input  logically it does nothing. Its purpose is electrical: it provides a clean, high current copy of a signal to drive many downstream loads without the source being overloaded. Buffers also isolate sections of a circuit so a disturbance on one side cannot propagate back to the other.
docs.ad94093655: Set if S; otherwise hold unless R clears it
docs.ae68068a38: In 74Sim: Open collector chips (e.g. 74LS05) are modelled with a SINK_ONLY drive state. The pin pulls toward GND when active and floats HIGH otherwise add a pull up resistor in the simulator exactly as you would in hardware.
docs.aea540b63f: Darlington sink arrays are the standard interface between low current logic outputs and higher current loads such as relays, solenoids, stepper motor coils, and incandescent lamps. The ULN2003 (seven channels) and ULN2803 (eight channels) are the classic 16/18 pin Darlington driver arrays; each channel includes a built in flyback diode (brought out to a common COM pin) to clamp the inductive kickback when a relay or motor coil turns off.
docs.b14c0aed14: The image below shows a 2-input OR gate with one input left dangling. You might expect the dangling pin to read LOW it isn't connected to anything, after all but in practice it can sit anywhere, and the gate's output is whatever the noise on that pin decides moment to moment.
docs.b2731456ee: The practical effect is noise immunity. If the input sits near the switching point and a noise spike pushes it past the threshold, the output changes. Without hysteresis, the slightest additional noise could immediately toggle it back, causing rapid oscillation (chatter). With hysteresis the output stays put: to reverse, the input must travel all the way to the opposite threshold. The Schmitt trigger is the standard circuit that adds hysteresis to a logic input.
docs.b3234cb291: A 74 series chip uses CMOS or TTL transistors internally to implement its logic function. Pins are numbered around the package starting from pin 1.
docs.b52c2f9edc: Pedagogical bridge from SR to D
docs.b60a961c43: Sequential chips (flip flops, counters, shift registers) do the same but additionally carry per instance state in a ffState map. Edge detection is performed by comparing the current clock input bit against a stored prevClk; a rising or falling edge is recognised exactly once per transition, even though the gate evaluator may be called many times per simulation step.
docs.b71054533d: Supply: 2 to 6V, suitable for battery powered designs.
docs.ba497cc81e: Pivoting matters here because real 74 series circuits mix wildly different impedances on the same matrix: a 100 kΩ pull up sits next to a 33 Ω LED bulk resistor, a 2.5 Ω VCC rail next to a 1 GΩ leak. Pivoting picks the largest available coefficient at each elimination step, which keeps floating point cancellation under control. (Gaussian elimination with partial pivoting)
docs.ba96cc973b: Propagation delay: ~10 ns. Max frequency: ~25 MHz.
docs.bb1ebd57e6: Tag power nodes: mark VCC (5 V) and GND (0 V); detect VCC‑GND shorts.
docs.be4d674582: Open collector output actively sinking current.
docs.be8eab6319: Static sensitivity: CMOS inputs are sensitive to ESD. Earth yourself before handling.
docs.c099602ec2: The name “transparent” describes its behavior from the outside: while EN = 1 the latch is transparent any change at D propagates straight through to Q after only the gate delay. When EN falls to 0 the latch “closes” and Q is frozen at whatever value D had at that moment. This is the standard meaning of “transparent latch” in digital logic textbooks; the two terms describe the same circuit.
docs.c0a1bc2978: A Darlington pair is two bipolar transistors wired so the first transistor's emitter current feeds directly into the second transistor's base. The result behaves like a single transistor with a current gain (β) equal to the product of the two individual gains often in the thousands. A few milliamps into the base can therefore switch hundreds of milliamps at the combined collector.
docs.c11f16dab0: The Set Reset (SR) latch is the simplest memory element. It has two inputs S (Set) and R (Reset) and two complementary outputs Q and /Q. Asserting S forces Q = 1; asserting R forces Q = 0; with both LOW the latch holds its previous value:
docs.c13c4572b3: It is best to build a circuit using a single logic family. If mixing is unavoidable, the power supply must be within the valid range of every family used.
docs.c3ae24b390: Conductance G = 1/R<sub>OUT</sub> from the pin's net to GND. The pin can only pull the net low.
docs.c45c34f20c: This is the kind of bug that survives every bench test and then fails the moment the board is mounted next to a motor or a fluorescent light. The gate isn't broken; an undriven input is just an antenna.
docs.c4a24526b1: An edge triggered SR flip flop exists conceptually it is a clocked SR latch whose response is restricted to the active clock edge but it inherits the forbidden S = R = 1 state and so is rarely manufactured as a discrete chip. Most designs that conceptually want an SR flip flop are built from a JK flip flop with the J = K = 1 input simply not used.
docs.c9a0a1040a: To get a usable HIGH level you must add an external pull up resistor between the output pin and VCC. The key advantage is wired AND: multiple open collector outputs can share the same wire, and the line is pulled LOW whenever any one of them is active. This makes open collector outputs ideal for bus lines and for driving loads (LEDs, relays) at a voltage higher than the chip’s own supply.
docs.cb27ec6c67: Outputs can sink up to 8 mA but source only ~0.4 mA.
docs.cb5d0a76d6: DIP (Dual In line Package) chips straddle the center channel. Each pin connects to its own 5-hole column, so you can attach up to 4 other wires or components to a single chip pin.
docs.cb67d3dd4a: Chaining N flip flops divides by 2N. A four bit binary counter (e.g. 74HC93) provides ÷2, ÷4, ÷8, and ÷16 outputs simultaneously from its Q0–Q3 outputs. Dedicated frequency divider chips like the 74HC92 (÷12) and the CD4060 (14 stage binary divider with built in oscillator) provide specific division ratios in a single package.
docs.cb80f1ecea: Search results only turn up distributor pages or application notes, not a pin level datasheet.
docs.cbb9a6a342: A small leak conductance of 10<sup>−9</sup> S is added to every diagonal entry of A. This prevents the matrix from going singular when a node has no real conductance path (for example, a wire segment connected to nothing) and costs nothing physically. 10<sup>−9</sup> S corresponds to 1 GΩ.
docs.cc23a44787: Conductance G = 1/R between the two end nodes i, j: A<sub>ii</sub> += G, A<sub>jj</sub> += G, A<sub>ij</sub> −= G, A<sub>ji</sub> −= G.
docs.cd3f3aa650: All threshold comparisons, output impedances, and pull up values are driven by the selected chip family.
docs.ce4117615e: Pass 0, assume all off. Every LED and diode is stamped as a near open circuit with conductance G<sub>off</sub> ≈ 1 μS (1 MΩ). Solve. Inspect each device's anode minus cathode voltage; any device with a positive value greater than its V<sub>F</sub> is forward biased.
docs.cfaaf22197: Before any math runs, the netlist builder converts the spatial layout into a graph of electrical nets using a BFS flood fill over connected holes:
docs.cfe02d7e68: Asynchronous clear is used at power up to put every flip flop in a known state before the first clock edge. Asynchronous preset is rarer in practice but appears in counter chips that need to load a non zero value as part of a parallel load operation. Because these inputs bypass the clock, signals connected to them must be debounced and free of glitches a single nanosecond pulse on /CLR will reset the flip flop.
docs.d050803b7f: OR: output is HIGH when any input is HIGH.
docs.d0d2a79705: Image credit: Audrius Meskauskas, Creative Commons
docs.d271dbb25b: 74HC combines the speed of TTL with the very low power consumption of CMOS. Pin arrangements are identical to 74LS but input thresholds differ, so a 74LS output cannot reliably drive a 74HC input.
docs.d32fd9bf75: SR latch, Ninja~commonswiki (talk | contribs), drawn by Heron 20:28, 5 Jul 2004 (UTC), via Wikimedia Commons. GFDL.
docs.d545f7fa71: Most circuits converge in 2 to 3 iterations. The 30 iteration cap exists to bound pathological cases like a free running NAND gate ring oscillator, where the simulator picks one consistent state for the snapshot rather than oscillating in place.
docs.d580edce08: So 74LS04 is a 74 series Low power Schottky hex inverter. See Chip Families for a full breakdown of family differences.
docs.d5844f6859: A 74 series chip can be oriented by the notch on one of its short sides. With the notch on the left (as in 74Sim):
docs.d8291d0c9c: When a chip is unverified, its pin names, pin count, and logic function in the simulator are an educated guess. Simulation results for these chips may be inaccurate.
docs.d8af75a01e: A frequency divider produces an output whose frequency is a fraction of its input clock frequency. The simplest divider is a single toggle flip flop: with J=K=1 (JK) or D=/Q (D type), Q toggles on every clock edge, producing an output at exactly half the input frequency a divide by-2.
docs.d9689f0bf2: A chip is marked unverified when no confirmed datasheet URL was available at the time the chip definition was written. This can happen when:
docs.d9db03355f: Drive states are kept across calls to the simulator's main evaluate() function, so the next pass starts with the previous step's drives already in place. This is essential for feedback paths like a ripple counter, where a chip's own output feeds back to its clock or enable pin.
docs.dab787fe78: 74HC and 74HCT (CMOS) inputs have no such bias. Their input impedance is essentially infinite, so a floating pin can sit at any voltage at all. Worse, if the voltage lingers near the threshold, both the n-channel and p-channel transistors in the input buffer conduct simultaneously, drawing tens of milliamps and heating the chip. CMOS datasheets are explicit on this point: every unused input must be tied to VCC or GND.
docs.dad6008cba: Wikipedia contributors, “Flip flop (electronics),” Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flip-flop_(electronics). (Accessed: May 4, 2026).
docs.dafcb451c9: No stamp; the pin is electrically disconnected. Open collector outputs in this state receive an implicit 4.7 kΩ pull up to VCC.
docs.dd9d41a50f: Capacitors are time derivative elements: their current depends on dV/dt, but MNA is a static linear system at one instant in time. SPICE's classic answer is the companion model: replace the capacitor with an equivalent resistor and current source that approximates its behaviour over a finite time step dt.
docs.dde112f7a8: Tosaka, CC BY 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons.
docs.de0a3d9f0c: The 74LS family uses Transistor to Transistor Logic (TTL) circuitry. It is fast but consumes more power than the CMOS families that followed it.
docs.deb91b31d1: Want to see what's possible first? Browse the Example Projects pre built circuits you can load and poke around in.
docs.dfd57f543a: BRMs are used wherever a programmable fraction of a clock is needed  stepper motor speed control, programmable clock dividers, and early digital to analog conversion schemes. Unlike a simple divider, the output pulses are not evenly spaced; they are distributed as uniformly as possible across the clock period.
docs.e04ceb4863: 74 series chips are logic chips designed to perform specific logical functions: AND, OR, NAND, flip flops, counters, and more. The number in the chip name determines its logical function; the letters indicate the family.
docs.e2a2d72db7: Normal totem pole output driving HIGH or LOW.
docs.e40d019e97: After each pass, the maximum voltage change across any capacitor is measured. If it exceeds 0.1 V, dt is halved (minimum 1 ms). If it is below 0.02 V, dt grows by 20% (maximum 50 ms). This adaptive scheme keeps the RC charging curve smooth without wasting CPU at steady state.
docs.e42d6dbd2a: Supply: 1.65 to 3.6V. Many parts have 5V tolerant inputs but cannot be powered from 5V.
docs.e4a5f9ece2: The characteristic equation of a flip flop expresses the next state Q(t+1) abbreviated Q+ as a Boolean function of the inputs and the current state Q. It is the equation you derive from the truth table by writing out each row that produces Q+ = 1, OR ing them together, and minimising. These equations are the starting point for synthesising a sequential circuit from a state diagram.
docs.e4bc6dc6bd: When a 74LS output needs to drive a 74HC input, connect a 2.2 kΩ pull up resistor between the +5V supply and the input pin.
docs.e51208ea12: New to 74 series chips? Read 74 Series Logic Chips, then Chip Families.
docs.e79ddfa629: Closed switches and pressed buttons are recorded as conducting pairs. Their endpoints stay on separate nets (so VCC/GND identity does not propagate through a switch), but the simulator stamps a near zero resistance (0.01 Ω) between them so voltage conducts freely.
docs.e8c79d6c2f: Every output pin is tagged with one of three drive states after each gate evaluation:
docs.eabbb96a40: Norton equivalent: G = 1/R<sub>OUT</sub> stamped on the pin's net, plus a current I = V<sub>drive</sub>/R<sub>OUT</sub> injected into z. R<sub>OUT</sub> depends on the chip family (714 Ω for 74LS, 150 Ω for 74HC/HCT, 50 Ω for 74LVC), limiting max drive current to ~7 mA, ~33 mA, or ~100 mA respectively.
docs.eb08186210: In the table below, · denotes AND, + denotes OR, ⊕ denotes XOR, and an apostrophe denotes NOT (so K′ is /K).
docs.ebf8ed7d53: 74LS (TTL) inputs tend to float HIGH. The bipolar input stage has a built in path to VCC through the input transistor, so an undriven pin pulls itself up to roughly 1.4 V above the LOW threshold, and usually read as a logic HIGH. This is the source of the classic bug: a forgotten wire silently enables a gate that should be disabled, the circuit "almost works", and you spend an afternoon chasing it.
docs.ecfcc024cc: 74Sim's analog core is a Modified Nodal Analysis (MNA) engine, the same mathematical foundation used by SPICE. The circuit is modelled as a single linear system:
docs.edd35473ce: The D latch also called a transparent latch eliminates the SR latch’s forbidden state by deriving R from S inside the chip: a single data input D drives S directly, and an inverter generates R = /D. Because S and R are now always opposites, the illegal S = R = 1 condition cannot occur. The latch has just two inputs: D (data) and EN (enable, sometimes labelled LE for “latch enable” or G for “gate”).
docs.ef01a3c631: In 74Sim: Chips with totem pole outputs (the majority of 74 series parts) are shown with a PUSH_PULL drive state in the simulator. Wiring two PUSH_PULL outputs together to the same node is flagged as a conflict, matching real world bus contention.
docs.efeaa43b22: Pass 0 (LED off, stamp Goff): The VCC rail stamps G = 0.4 S to net 1 with current I = 2 A into z1. The 330 Ω resistor stamps G = 1/330 ≈ 0.00303 S between nets 1 and 2. The LED stamps Goff = 10−6 S between net 2 and GND. Adding the leak conductance:
docs.f1d988a5e0: A binary rate multiplier (BRM) generates an output pulse train whose average frequency is proportional to a binary input word. If the input word is N and the clock is f, the output rate is approximately N/2n × f, where n is the word width. The 74LS97 is the classic 6 bit BRM chip.
docs.f211437868: Clock components read the wall clock time (performance.now()) to toggle their output at the user-specified frequency, independent of the simulation time step.
docs.f3d529de39: Norton source pulling the pin's net toward V<sub>drive</sub> (0 V or 5 V) through R<sub>OUT</sub>.
docs.f4175d63ff: Wikipedia contributors, “Binary multiplier,” Wikipedia, The Free Encyclopedia. [Online]. Available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_multiplier. (Accessed: May 4, 2026).
docs.f59d696f46: where x is the unknown vector of node voltages, A is the conductance matrix, and z is the injected current vector. GND is the reference node (index 0, excluded from the matrix), so for a circuit with N+1 nets the matrix is N×N.
docs.f6f6344894: Resistors and chip outputs: I = (V<sub>a</sub> − V<sub>b</sub>) × G, where G is the branch conductance from the stamp. For a Norton stamped chip output, the effective branch current is I = (V<sub>drive</sub> − V<sub>pin</sub>) / R<sub>OUT</sub>.
docs.f73b0a437d: XOR (exclusive OR): output is HIGH when inputs differ. Used in adders and parity checkers.
docs.f7752efb8f: A gated SR latch adds an enable (or clock) input EN. The S and R inputs are AND ed with EN before reaching the cross coupled core, so the latch responds to S and R only while EN = 1 and holds its state while EN = 0. Gating turns the bare SR latch into something usable in a clocked system: changes propagate only during the active phase of EN.
docs.f7e6b5dde8: All latches share the same internal structure: two cross coupled inverting gates (NOR or NAND) form a positive feedback loop that holds either Q = 1, /Q = 0 or Q = 0, /Q = 1. The four common variants (SR, gated SR, D, gated D) differ only in how external inputs steer that loop.
docs.fa3bbb9e9f: How to spot an unverified chip
docs.fabf220698: Fan out: one output drives up to 10 74LS inputs.
docs.facf6efba2: Fan out: 50+ 74HC inputs, but only 10 74LS inputs.
docs.fb84392a60: The T (Toggle) flip flop has a single input T. When T = 0 the flip flop holds; when T = 1 it toggles Q on every active clock edge. It is functionally a JK flip flop with J and K tied together (J = K = T), or equivalently a D flip flop with D = T XOR Q.
docs.fd334d9d00: Common 74 series D flip flops include the 74HC74 (dual D with async preset and clear), the 74HC175 (quad D with shared clock and clear), the 74HC273 (octal D with shared clock and clear), and the 74HC374 (octal D with shared clock and tristate /OE). Inside CPUs and FPGAs, almost every register bit is a D flip flop.
docs.fe044603a6: A Darlington sink is an open collector output built from a Darlington pair instead of a single transistor. Like any open collector stage it can only pull its output toward GND (sink current); it never sources current or drives the pin HIGH. The load current path - and the diode reverse voltage flyback diodes must withstand on inductive loads - flows from the external supply, through the load, into the OUT pin, and to ground through the Darlington pair.
